title: Interview Transcript
study: Social Integration and Academic Divergence: An Analysis of International Student Experiences at HKUST(GZ)

background_and_general_perspective.question: Could you briefly introduce yourself — your program, year of study, and how long have you been at HKUST(GZ)?
background_and_general_perspective.answer: My name is Younger. I am a freshman at HKUST(GZ), originally from Hunan Province in central-northern China. In terms of extracurricular involvement, I have participated in Robomaster, an English speech competition where I won a national silver award, and ColdMap, a mathematics competition. I am currently taking on a WestLab and HP role within the IGM project.

background_and_general_perspective.question: How would you describe the general social environment at HKUST(GZ)?
background_and_general_perspective.answer: I would describe it as quite simple and genuinely friendly. Because the university is relatively small, you tend to see the same faces every day, which makes the environment feel familiar and comfortable. When I competed in an English speech competition held at Jinan University — a much larger campus — I noticed how a bigger crowd can easily trigger social anxiety. That kind of pressure is much lower here. Overall, I think the social environment at HKUST(GZ) is approachable and low-stress.

interaction_with_international_students.question: How would you describe your interactions with international students at HKUST(GZ)?
interaction_with_international_students.answer: I think I basically know most of the international students here. Personally, I am quite open — I enjoy talking to people from different parts of the world and exploring different cultures. I have made some genuinely deep connections. For example, Gani and I grew quite close last semester through our shared participation in an English speech competition. I also greet many international students on a daily basis. Generally, my interactions with international students have been very positive and frequent.

interaction_with_international_students.question: Do you think it is easy or difficult for Chinese and international students to become friends here? Why?
interaction_with_international_students.answer: I think it depends on the individual. If someone is introverted or not confident in their spoken English, it will probably be more difficult for them to approach international students. My rough estimate is that for around 70% of Chinese students, forming deep connections with international students is somewhat challenging. That said, I have also seen introverted students with limited English make a conscious decision to push themselves — walking up to international students, starting conversations, and practising their oral English. I find that kind of effort both admirable and effective.

interaction_with_international_students.question: In your experience, do Chinese and international students mix naturally, or do they mostly stay in separate circles? Why?
interaction_with_international_students.answer: I think they mostly stay in separate circles. From what I observe, most Chinese students tend to speak Chinese when discussing problems or working together. When you use a language that others cannot understand, you effectively build a wall that excludes anyone outside that group. Personally, I try to avoid doing that — I would not prefer to speak a language restricted to a small subset of people when others are present. The most important factor driving this separation is, I think, language.

interaction_with_international_students.question: What factors make interaction between Chinese and international students easier?
interaction_with_international_students.answer: I think the barrier between the two groups is actually thinner than it appears. The moment you are willing to take the first step — even just saying 'hi' or striking up a brief conversation — things tend to open up naturally from there. Willingness and initiative are really the main enablers.

interaction_with_international_students.question: What factors make interaction harder?
interaction_with_international_students.answer: The main barrier is a lack of self-confidence among Chinese students. I had a roommate who was not very confident in his spoken English, but who genuinely wanted to get to know international students and learn about other parts of the world. He simply could not bring himself to take that first step. I remember during a summer camp, he asked me to approach a student from Greece on his behalf just to ask about the weather there. It was a little funny, but it really illustrates how self-confidence — more than ability — is what holds many Chinese students back.

interaction_with_international_students.question: To what extent do language or cultural differences affect communication between Chinese and international students?
interaction_with_international_students.answer: Personally, I do not think cultural differences are a significant barrier to communication — they are more of an interesting dimension to explore than an obstacle. Language is still the primary factor. That said, even without fluent English, I believe basic mutual understanding is possible through individual words, gestures, and context. Language can be a barrier, but it is not insurmountable. I would say it is present but not that serious.

social_integration_and_academic_impact.question: In your opinion, does social integration affect international students' academic experiences? How?
social_integration_and_academic_impact.answer: I think it does, particularly in areas involving communication. For example, I know Izat — a student born in Kyrgyzstan who studied in Russia before coming to China — and his cross-cultural background gives him an exceptionally rich and distinctive perspective. During a public speaking course, I noticed that his contributions carried a depth and originality that most students in the room could not reach. I think that kind of integrated social experience clearly enriches communication-based academic work. Whether it has a deep effect on STEM courses is less clear to me.

social_integration_and_academic_impact.question: Have you noticed any difference in your academic performance between UCUG and UFUG courses? Why?
social_integration_and_academic_impact.answer: Yes, there is quite a noticeable difference. In my UCUG Intercultural Communication course, I was grouped with two international students — one from Taiwan and Tony from Korea — and we collaborated very effectively. There were no significant obstacles in communicating or brainstorming, and we produced strong results for both the midterm drama and the final project. In contrast, in UFUG courses like C++, where individual technical ability matters far more than communication, I do not perform as well. The contrast between the two is quite clear.

social_integration_and_academic_impact.question: From what you have observed, do international students seem to perform differently in UCUG and UFUG courses? Why?
social_integration_and_academic_impact.answer: In courses where communication contributes significantly to the final grade, international students tend to do noticeably better than many Chinese students. However, this distinction does not strictly follow the UCUG/UFUG divide. For instance, I heard that a student named Leisya performed very well in a UFUG biology course that involved extensive report writing — which is also a form of communication. In courses like Chemistry in Everyday Life or Economics, where the focus is primarily on knowledge, I do not see much difference between the two groups. As for STEM courses where some Chinese students score highly, I think that is largely because we already covered that material in high school. When both groups encounter genuinely new content, the performance gap tends to be minimal.

social_integration_and_academic_impact.question: Do you think international students sometimes face disadvantages in group work, class participation, or access to academic support because of weaker social connections? Why or why not?
social_integration_and_academic_impact.answer: I think it depends on the group composition. If a group consists mainly of international students, there is unlikely to be a problem. But if there is only one or two international students in a group of eight or nine Chinese students — especially if those students are not comfortable speaking English and tend to be introverted — that can create a very significant disadvantage. It varies case by case.

social_integration_and_academic_impact.question: Have you noticed any differences in how Chinese and international students participate in group projects, study groups, or classroom interaction?
social_integration_and_academic_impact.answer: There are some differences, though I think they are more individual than group-based. In my Intercultural Communication group, the active members — myself, Tony, Liloa from Taiwan, and one other Chinese student — drove most of the work, while the remaining two Chinese students were more passive, mostly completing assigned tasks rather than taking initiative. I would not frame this as 'international students are active, Chinese students are passive' — the active group included both. The more passive members were predominantly Chinese, and I think this partly traces back to the high school education system in China, which focuses heavily on exam preparation rather than independent decision-making or initiative. But ultimately, it varies between individuals.

social_integration_and_academic_impact.question: Do you think students with stronger social networks generally perform better academically at HKUST(GZ)? Why?
social_integration_and_academic_impact.answer: I actually think it works the other way around. I believe students who perform well academically tend to attract better social networks — particularly among Chinese students, who often gravitate towards high achievers. I see this as a bias reinforced by the education system. Personally, academic performance is not one of my criteria for choosing friends, and I hope I do not hold that kind of bias. I think genuine friendships can be built with anyone, based on personality and shared interests.

institutional_and_structural_factors.question: What do you think are the biggest barriers preventing stronger integration between Chinese and international students?
institutional_and_structural_factors.answer: I would say it is the lack of shared experiences. If Chinese and international students had more joint activities, group projects, or shared challenges, they would naturally grow closer. But most of the time, Chinese students tend to form groups among themselves, and that self-sorting is one of the main obstacles to building deeper cross-cultural connections.

institutional_and_structural_factors.question: Do you think the university does enough to encourage interaction and inclusion between different student groups? Why or why not?
institutional_and_structural_factors.answer: I do not think the effort is sufficient. The university provides a platform, but it does not take active steps to link different student groups — particularly for those who want to reach out but lack the confidence or a concrete opportunity to do so. The university acts as a facilitator in the broadest sense, but it is not functioning as an active connector between different student communities.

institutional_and_structural_factors.question: Are there any university practices, campus habits, or classroom dynamics that unintentionally make integration harder?
institutional_and_structural_factors.answer: Yes. Most of the activities organised by the Residential College are centred on Chinese festivals and traditions, but there is nothing equivalent for international students' cultural occasions. For example, Kazakh New Year falls in late March, and the university made no announcement, held no event, and offered no acknowledgment of it whatsoever. I think that kind of oversight — however unintentional — sends a signal that international students' cultures are not considered here. Even a brief message or small gesture would have made a difference. Personally, I found that quite disappointing.

reflection_and_recommendations.question: What do you think HKUST(GZ) could do better to improve integration between Chinese and international students?
reflection_and_recommendations.answer: The most effective approach I can think of is organising cultural activities that bring students from different backgrounds together around shared experiences. For example, around Kazakh New Year, the university could host a banquet featuring traditional Kazakh food, open to both Kazakh and Chinese students. A gesture like that would simultaneously celebrate international students' traditions and serve as a genuine gateway for Chinese students to discover and engage with other cultures. I think events built around diversity and integration — rather than passive coexistence — would make it much easier for students to connect across backgrounds.

reflection_and_recommendations.question: Is there anything about this issue that people often misunderstand or fail to notice?
reflection_and_recommendations.answer: I think the most common failure is not misunderstanding, but simply not noticing. Most Chinese students are unaware of when international students' important cultural moments occur — most do not even know that Kazakh New Year exists, for example. That unawareness is a quiet but real form of exclusion. International students experience moments where their culture is simply invisible to those around them, and I think that goes largely unnoticed by the Chinese student community.

end_note: Transcript cleaned and organised. Original audio recording retained for verification.
